text: Last class you presented how the product is made.
Today the client says it is too expensive. You handle it.

We love the product, but honestly, the price is a real concern. It is over our budget.
I understand your concern. You could try our smaller model -- it is more affordable but still very reliable.
Hmm, we hesitate a little. We really want the bigger engine, but not at that price.
Then we should look at the volume together. For a bigger order, we could offer a better price -- a more reasonable one.
That sounds fair. But we also need it faster. Can you do anything about delivery?
Let me reassure you. We could adjust the delivery date. I think we can reach a fair compromise.

I think we can reach a fair compromise.

Hi Roberto, this is Amy. I am calling about the client from the Chinese company. After your factory tour, they liked the product, but they have a concern about the price. They think it is too expensive for their budget. So today the call will be about handling objections. Here is my advice. Never argue with the client. When they object, listen first, then make a polite suggestion. Use should and could, not strong words. For example, you could say: you could try our smaller model, it is more affordable. Or: we should look at the budget together and find an alternative. If the volume is high, you could offer a better price. Stay calm, be reasonable, and always give the client an option. Do not just say no. Give them a way forward. Good luck on the call, and let me know how it goes.

This is a quick summary of the client call today. The client had a concern about the price. They said it was too high for their budget. I did not argue. I listened to the objection first, and then I made some polite suggestions. I told them they could try the smaller model, which is more affordable. I also said we should look at the volume together, because for a bigger order I could offer a better price. They hesitated at first, but in the end they felt the offer was reasonable. We agreed on a small compromise: I adjusted the delivery date and they accepted the price. I think the polite approach worked well. I will send Amy the details tonight.

First, listen carefully to the client's objection. Then, show that you understand the concern. After that, make a polite suggestion with should or could. Next, offer an alternative, like a smaller model or a better price. Finally, agree on a fair compromise so both sides are happy.
